1	# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market
2	
3	This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.
4	
5	## Introduction: AI, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and Labor Markets
6	
7	Artificial intelligence is the general-purpose technology at the core of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and its restructuring of labor markets is already empirically measurable: U.S. job postings in occupations with above-median AI-substitution scores fell by approximately 20% relative to below-median occupations within three years of ChatGPT's November 2022 release.[1][2][3] The term "Fourth Industrial Revolution"—also called Industry 4.0 or 4IR—was coined by Klaus Schwab, founder and executive chairman of the World Economic Forum, and describes a world built on the digital Third Industrial Revolution in which individuals move between digital domains and offline reality through connected technology.[4] Within this transformation, AI is characterized as the "new electricity" of the emerging industrial revolution, with deep learning expected to transform most industries and become indispensable to a wide range of businesses and organizations.[5] One review frames the 4IR technology cluster of smart technology, artificial intelligence, robotics, and algorithms (STARA) as predicted to replace a third of the jobs that exist today, with forecasts that 85 million jobs may be displaced by 2025 while 97 million new roles emerge that are more adapted to the new division of labor between humans, machines, and algorithms.[6][2] What distinguishes AI from earlier digital technologies is that, as a general-purpose technology touching nearly every sector and occupation, generative AI does not merely automate routine, noncognitive tasks but creates novel content—text, images, video, and audio—indistinguishable from human-created content.[1] Generative AI is diffusing at a pace and scale rarely observed in prior general-purpose technologies: U.S. private investment in AI reached $109.1 billion in 2024—nearly twelve times higher than China's $9.3 billion—and 78% of organizations reported using AI in 2024, up from 55% in 2023.[2] ChatGPT became, in one industry analysis, the fastest-growing consumer application in history with more than 100 million active users in 2 months, and 39.4% of Americans had used generative AI by August 2024—a penetration within one year nearly double that of personal computers three years after launch.[7][2] The economic stakes are correspondingly large, with generative AI estimated to contribute between $2.6 trillion and $4.4 trillion annually to the global economy and to raise annual global productivity by 0.2 per cent to 3.3 per cent depending on adoption rates.[8] Exposure estimates converge on sharp occupational heterogeneity: the ILO finds clerical work is the only broad occupational group highly exposed to generative AI, with 24 per cent of clerical tasks highly exposed and an additional 58 per cent at medium exposure, while other occupational groups show only 1 to 4 per cent of tasks highly exposed.[9][2] Potential automation effects are concentrated in richer economies—5.5 per cent of employment in high-income countries versus 0.4 per cent in low-income countries—while augmentation could affect 13.4 per cent of employment in high-income and 10.4 per cent in low-income countries, with more than double the share of women potentially affected by automation.[9] This gender gradient is corroborated by estimates that 7.8% of women's jobs in high-income nations, totaling around 21 million jobs, could be automated, compared with 2.9% of men's employment, or around 9 million positions.[10] Industry-level exposure is similarly uneven, with U.S. finance and insurance at 58.02% exposure to AI language modeling while agriculture, forestry, fishing and hunting sits at 7.2827%, and national exposure scores around 0.50 implying that demand for half of all workplace abilities may shift.[7] Applying Frey and Osborne's automation-probability methodology to a survey of 341 employees in Saudi Arabia, one study found approximately 47% work in jobs vulnerable to some degree of technological replacement, a share that drops to 15% when analyzed at the task level—illustrating how measurement granularity reshapes estimated disruption.[11] Industry estimates suggest generative AI could automate 29.5% of the hours worked in the U.S. economy by 2030, up from 21.5% without it, while the OECD finds that occupations most at risk of automation account for an average of 27% of the workforce across its member countries.[10][1] Forecasts of net employment effects diverge sharply: one Goldman Sachs analysis reports generative AI could automate up to 300 million full-time jobs in the U.S. and Europe, while Goldman Sachs Research in August 2025 estimates unemployment will rise only half a percentage point during the transition with 2.5% of U.S. employment at risk of related job loss, and a Forrester projection anticipates 2.4 million U.S. jobs lost by 2030 but 11.08 million jobs affected or transformed.[10][12] Some analysts are more sanguine still, arguing that AI is unlikely to be a direct threat to the wages and employment of U.S. workers and that outcomes hinge on restoring labor-market power balance rather than on AI policy specifics, while the U.S.[13] Bureau of Labor Statistics expects AI over 2023–33 to primarily affect occupations whose core tasks can be most easily replicated by generative AI in its current form, with trajectories for computer, legal, business and financial, and architecture and engineering occupations remaining uncertain.[14] The speed of current disruption contrasts with prior automation waves, in which one additional robot per thousand workers reduced the U.S. employment-to-population ratio by only 0.18–0.34 percentage points between 1990 and 2007, and earlier AI exposure had no discernible impact on employment or wages during 2010–2018.[3] Distributional risks also extend across borders, as informal employment represents 89% of total employment in low-income countries compared with 15.9% in high-income countries, conditioning how AI shocks propagate through developing labor markets.[15] This review therefore organizes the evidence around the three channels emphasized in the literature—job displacement, augmentation, and creation—tracing their heterogeneous incidence across occupations, industries, countries, and worker groups.[8][9][3]
8	
9	## Theoretical Foundations: Task-Based Models of Displacement and Reinstatement
10	
11	The task-based framework's central equilibrium result is that automation's net effect on labor demand is indeterminate a priori: it equals the sum of a productivity effect that raises demand for labor in non-automated tasks and a displacement effect that always reduces the labor share of value added, meaning automation can reduce labor demand even as it raises productivity.[16] The displacement effect arises because automation enables capital to substitute for labor across a range of tasks—historically in artisan spinning and weaving and in agricultural mechanization, and currently via industrial robots in machining, welding, painting, and assembling and via specialized software and artificial intelligence replacing tasks in accounting, sales, logistics, trading, and some managerial occupations.[16] Counterbalancing displacement is the reinstatement effect—the creation of new tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage, such as programming, software and app development, database design and analysis, and computer-security tasks—which directly increases both the labor share and labor demand.[16] Reinstatement has been quantitatively first-order: about half of US employment growth over 1980–2015 took place in occupations in which job titles or tasks performed by workers changed.[16] The equilibrium balance has nonetheless shifted against labor in recent decades, as the sharp slowdown in US wage-bill growth over the last three decades reflects weaker-than-usual productivity growth combined with significant task-content shifts against labor, with stronger displacement effects and considerably weaker reinstatement effects during the last 30 years than in the decades before.[16] The framework implies it is not "brilliant" automation but "so-so technologies"—such as automated customer service, whose productivity gains are too small to offset displacement—that threaten employment and wages, and that automation by itself tends to produce slower wage growth than productivity growth.[16] A complementary mechanism explains why displacement has not eliminated work: tasks that cannot be substituted by automation are generally complemented by it, as when ATMs quadrupled from approximately 100,000 to 400,000 between 1995 and 2010 while US bank teller employment rose modestly from 500,000 to approximately 550,000 over 1980–2010, because tellers per branch fell by more than a third between 1988 and 2004 but the number of urban branches rose by more than 40 percent and tellers shifted toward "relationship banking".[17] The routine/non-routine task boundary has historically defined displacement, with computers substituting for workers in routine, codifiable tasks while amplifying human comparative advantage in problem-solving, adaptability, and creativity—a substitution propelled by the cost of performing a standardized set of computations falling by at least 1.7 trillion-fold since the manual computing era.[17] Generative AI pushes the automation frontier into non-routine cognitive tasks: around 80% of the US workforce could have at least 10% of their work tasks affected by large language models and approximately 19% could see at least 50% of tasks impacted, with about 15% of all worker tasks completable significantly faster using an LLM alone and between 47 and 56% when incorporating LLM-powered software and tooling.[18][19][20] Even at this expanded frontier, exposure estimates imply augmentation dominates full automation at the occupation level: one global analysis finds clerical work is the only broad occupational category highly exposed to generative AI, with 5.5 percent of employment in high-income countries potentially exposed to automation effects versus 0.4 percent in low-income countries, while 13.4 percent versus 10.4 percent has augmentation potential.[21] Firm-level task evidence sharpens the displacement margin: when AI can perform most of the tasks that make up a job, that role's share of employment within a company falls by about 14%, but when AI's impact is concentrated in a few tasks, employment in the role can grow—and even workers in high-wage, heavily AI-exposed roles saw their share of total employment grow by about 3% over five years, with large increases in AI use linked to about 6% higher employment growth and 9.5% more sales growth over five years.[22] Experimental evidence shows how within-job task content restructures before employment adjusts: access to ChatGPT reduced time taken on professional writing tasks by 0.8 standard deviations and raised output quality by 0.4 standard deviations, while the share of time spent rough-drafting fell by more than half and the share spent editing more than doubled, indicating the technology mostly substitutes for worker effort rather than complementing worker skills.[23][24] Where tasks are fully contestable by generative AI, market-level displacement is already measurable: freelancers in highly affected occupations experienced a 2% decrease in monthly jobs and a 5.2% decrease in monthly earnings after ChatGPT's release, were 1.2 percentage points less likely to receive any employment in a given month, and took 4.7% fewer jobs conditional on employment, with image-based generative AI producing near-identical reductions of 2.1% in jobs and 5.2% in earnings.[25][26][25] Job-posting data confirm displacement scales with task-substitution vulnerability: postings for occupations with above-median AI substitution scores fell by an average of 12 percent relative to below-median occupations, with the effect rising from 6 percent in the first year after ChatGPT's launch to 18 percent by the third year, concentrated in entry-level positions requiring neither advanced degrees (18 percent) nor extensive experience (20 percent) and in administrative support (40 percent) and professional services (30 percent).[27] The displacement-versus-reinstatement split appears directly in firm hiring behavior, as firms reduce job postings in automation-prone occupations while increasing postings in augmentation-prone occupations following ChatGPT's introduction.[28] Aggregate equilibrium outcomes remain muted so far: LLM adoption among US workers rose rapidly from 30.1% as of December 2024 to 38.3% as of December 2025, yet analyses find only small wage effects in more exposed occupations and no significant effects on job openings or total jobs.[29][30] Consistent with that pattern, actual AI usage remains a fraction of theoretical capability, there is no systematic increase in unemployment for highly exposed workers since late 2022, and each 10 percentage point increase in observed-exposure coverage is associated with a 0.6 percentage point lower BLS growth projection, alongside suggestive evidence that hiring of younger workers has slowed in exposed occupations.[31] The reinstatement channel is also geographically uneven in ways that bias the global equilibrium toward displacement: among 441.8 million jobs worldwide in categories that could leverage generative AI for augmentation, 66.9 million (15.1 percent) lack internet access, implying developing countries may experience the disruptive effects of generative AI faster than its productivity benefits.[32] Finally, consistent with the task-based prediction that the direction of wage changes is theoretically ambiguous, workers so far report experiencing AI mainly through changes in the tasks they perform within their current roles, with one analysis finding a one-standard-deviation increase in AI exposure associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth and the AI-skilled workforce still at most 0.3% of employment across OECD countries.[33]
12	
13	## Occupational Exposure: Quantifying Susceptibility to AI and Generative AI
14	
15	Task-based exposure indices converge on an occupational landscape in which generative AI's reach is broad but inverted relative to earlier automation: one in four workers globally is in an occupation with some GenAI exposure, and the most exposed are highly educated, highly paid white-collar and clerical workers rather than the routine and manual employees transformed by previous waves.[34][35][36] The foundational occupation-level approach estimated computerisation probabilities for 702 detailed US occupations using a Gaussian process classifier and related those probabilities to wages and educational attainment.[37] That routine-era baseline matters because routine employment had already been contracting for decades: between the 1980s and the 2020s, the US employment share of cognitive routine occupations fell from 27.72% to 19.47% (a 29.75% decline) and manual routine occupations from 26.44% to 20.88% (a 21.01% decline), while cognitive nonroutine occupations rose from 29.51% to 43.04%.[38] Felten, Raj and Seamans created and validated the AI Occupational Exposure (AIOE) and extended it to industry-level (AIIE) and county-level (AIGE) measures, an ability-based index on which computer programmers score 1.272 against 0.034 for manicurists and pedicurists.[39][40][41] Applying an LLM-specific task framework with both human and LLM annotation, Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin and Rock estimate that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces and general training, a share that jumps to just over 46% of jobs once current and likely future complementary software developments are included.[42] Under that framework a task is "exposed" if an LLM reduces completion time by 50 percent or more — assigning computer programmers an exposure score of 0.9 — and the authors argue that GPTs possess key characteristics of general-purpose technologies.[41][42] Extending this line to generative AI specifically, Felten, Raj and Seamans find that highly-educated, highly-paid, white-collar occupations may be most exposed to advances in language modeling and image generation, with exposure varying demographically.[35] Statistics Canada's complementarity-adjusted AIOE (built on Pizzinelli et al. 2023 and Felten, Raj and Seamans 2021) indicates that about 60% of Canadian employees could be exposed to AI-related job transformation, distributed in May 2021 as 4.2 million (31%) in high-exposure/low-complementarity jobs, 3.9 million (29%) in high-exposure/high-complementarity jobs and 5.4 million (40%) in low-exposure jobs.[36] The Canadian gradient rises with education — 37% of bachelor's-degree employees sit in the high-exposure/low-complementarity group versus 25% of employees with high school or less, 62% of whom are in low-exposure jobs — and sectorally 57% of employees in professional, scientific and technical services face high exposure with low complementarity against 16% in manufacturing (where 64% are low-exposure), while educational services is dominated by high complementarity at 69%.[36] The ILO's refined 2025 global index — drawing on 52,558 assessments from 1,640 workers covering 2,861 tasks sampled from the 29,753-task Polish occupational classification, triangulated with LLM predictions and Delphi-style expert review — sorts occupations into four progressively increasing exposure gradients and finds clerical occupations continue to have the highest exposure levels.[34] That index places 3.3% of global employment in the highest exposure category — 4.7% of female versus 2.4% of male employment, widening to 9.6% versus 3.5% in Gradient 4 in high-income countries — with overall exposure rising from 11% of employment in low-income countries to 34% in high-income countries.[34] For Europe, the Joint Research Centre traces exposure from 2008 to 2024 by linking 352 AI benchmarks to 14 cognitive abilities, 108 work tasks and 127 ISCO-3 occupations, finding an exponential increase in AI exposure across all occupational categories, concentrated in ideas-related abilities such as attention and search, comprehension and expression, and logical reasoning, with high-skilled occupations more exposed than elementary occupations.[43] A patent-based alternative, the AI Impact measure, uses deep-learning natural language processing to match 24,758 AI patents filed with the USPTO between 2015 and 2022 against 17,879 task descriptions across 759 O*NET occupations, finding impacts tied to specific skills — including nonroutine tasks such as diagnosing health conditions, programming computers and tracking flight routes — with cardiovascular technologists and technicians ranked first (AII 0.64) and many affected occupations, such as neurologists, software engineers and air traffic controllers, augmented rather than replaced in labor-shortage sectors including IT, healthcare and transport.[44] Applying Eloundou et al.'s task classifications to US employment, the Penn Wharton Budget Model puts exposure to AI automation at 75.5% of tasks in Office and Administrative Support Occupations, 68.4% in Business and Financial Operations, 62.6% in Computer and Mathematical and 60.1% in Sales and Related Occupations, against 2.6% in Building and Grounds Cleaning and Maintenance Occupations, and estimates that 40 percent of current GDP could be substantially affected.[45] On the earnings gradient the sources diverge in shape: the Penn Wharton analysis finds exposure peaking around the 80th percentile of earnings — with around half of those occupations' work susceptible — and the highest-earning occupations less exposed, while a US Treasury analysis finds the highly exposed share rising from 17.3% of workers earning under $25,000 to 36.1% of workers earning $100,000 or more.[45][46] The Treasury study further reports 25.4% of US workers highly exposed overall, 31.3% among bachelor's-degree holders, AI exposure four times greater in the most exposed PUMAs than in the least exposed (the District of Columbia highest among states at 39.8%), and a 0.94 correlation between PUMA-level AI exposure and the share of jobs that are teleworkable.[46] Usage-based novel datasets diverge sharply from capability-based indices: linking Claude conversations to O*NET tasks, Anthropic researchers estimate computer programmers' exposure at 0.061 — 6.1% of all queries — versus the 0.9 capability-based score for the same occupation.[41] Despite these high measured exposures, the ILO concludes that transformation of jobs is the most likely impact of GenAI because most occupations consist of tasks that require human input, and Yale's Budget Lab finds the exposure distribution stable — the lowest and medium exposure groups each comprising around 45% of workers and the highest only around 2% — with no sign so far that exposure, automation or augmentation measures are related to changes in employment or unemployment.[34][47] Methodologically, a critical survey cautions that task-exposure models treat AI as a collection of separate tools and that AIOE-style indices do not distinguish AI types, whereas GenAI's applicability to new tasks across many contexts marks a broader technological shift.[48]
16	
17	## Productivity and Augmentation: Evidence from Firms and Controlled Experiments
18	
19	Generative AI raises worker productivity by roughly 14% to 56% across randomized experiments and real firm deployments, with the largest gains accruing to lower-ability and less-experienced workers and therefore compressing—rather than widening—productivity inequality.[49][50][51][52][53] In the preregistered experiment by Noy and Zhang published in Science, 453 college-educated professionals randomly exposed to ChatGPT on occupation-specific writing tasks completed them in 40% less time with 18% higher output quality, and exposed workers were 2 times as likely to report using ChatGPT in their real job two weeks later and 1.6 times as likely two months later.[52] The working-paper version of that study—which reports 444 participants rather than 453—quantifies the effect as a 0.8 standard deviation reduction in time taken and a 0.4 standard deviation rise in quality, and finds that ChatGPT mostly substitutes for worker effort rather than complementing worker skills while restructuring tasks toward idea-generation and editing and away from rough-drafting.[51] In a staggered deployment of a generative-AI assistant across 5,179 customer-support agents and 3 million chats, access to AI recommendations raised resolutions per hour by 13.8% under the preferred specification, cut average handle time by 3.8 minutes (a 9% decline), increased chats handled per hour by roughly 14%, and produced only a small 1.3 percentage point rise in resolution rates from an 82% baseline with no economically significant change in customer satisfaction.[50] Those gains concentrated among lower-skilled and newer agents—a pattern the authors note contrasts with the skill-biased technical change documented for earlier waves of computer technology, with AI assistance potentially even decreasing conversation quality among the most skilled agents.[50] Independent industry analysis estimates generative AI could ultimately raise customer-service productivity by 30% to 50% once implemented at scale.[54] Large-scale randomized field experiments at a leading cross-border online retail platform provide the firm-level counterpart: integrating GenAI into seven consumer-facing workflows over six months in 2023–2024 raised sales by 0% to 16.3% depending on the application, and because inputs and prices were held constant these gains map directly into total factor productivity improvements, implying approximately $5 in annual incremental value per consumer across the four effective applications, with smaller and newer sellers and less experienced consumers benefiting disproportionately.[53] Other controlled trials converge on sizable micro-level effects, including a 56% speed increase for JavaScript programming with GitHub Copilot, a 26% increase in task completion rates in software development with Copilot, a 17% increase in job starts and 18% increase in retention when generative-AI voice agents conducted job interviews, and a 12% increase in task completion with 25% greater speed for consultants using GPT-4.[49][45] Nationally representative November 2024 survey data translate such experimental gains into worker-reported outcomes: weekly generative-AI users reported average time savings of 5.4% of work hours—2.2 hours for a 40-hour week—implying workers are 33% more productive in each AI-assisted hour and an estimated 1.1% increase in aggregate productivity.[55] Time savings scale with usage intensity (a 10 percentage point rise in the share of hours using AI is associated with a 1.7 percentage point rise in time saved), ranging from computer and mathematics workers, who used AI in nearly 12% of hours and saved 2.5% of work time, down to personal-service workers at 1.3% of hours and 0.4% saved, with information services recording the highest industry usage (14.0% of hours) and savings (2.6%).[55] A synthesis of this literature finds that, on average, AI tools boost individual productivity by around 30%, with some task-specific studies reporting improvements exceeding 50%, and a survey of more than 100,000 workers across 11 GenAI-exposed occupations found workers estimated ChatGPT could halve working time for one-third of their job tasks.[56][57] For pre-generative-AI firm adoption, estimates disagree: one event-study analysis finds firms innovating in AI raised sales per worker by 6.8% and a second study puts the figure at 4.4%, while another firm-level analysis finds no increase in sales per worker at all, prompting one review to bound the effect at between 0 and 6.8%.[58] On the augmentation-versus-automation margin, that same review reports that in high-income countries 13.4% of employment is exposed to augmentation by AI against only 5.5% exposed to automation, compared with 10.4% and 0.4% respectively in low-income countries.[58] Adoption remains the binding constraint on realizing these gains: only 5.4% of firms had adopted AI as of February 2024, up from 3.7% in September 2023, though Federal Reserve monitoring reports adoption reached roughly 18% of firms by year-end 2025, with about 41% of individuals using generative AI for work and 78% of the labor force employed at AI-adopting firms.[55][59][60] Firms' own reports suggest augmentation currently dominates displacement, with 84.6% of businesses saying AI replaced only a small number of tasks in the six months to February 2024, 94.6% reporting no change in employment, and expected employment effects roughly symmetric (6.5% increase versus 6.1% decrease), consistent with assessments finding no evidence yet of widespread generative-AI-driven job loss even in the most exposed occupations.[59][61] The central tension in this literature is therefore between large, well-identified micro-level gains and still-invisible macro effects: one projection estimates generative AI added just 0.01 percentage points to total factor productivity growth in 2025, peaking near 0.2 percentage points in 2032 and lifting TFP levels 1.5% by 2035 and 3.7% by 2075, while a European review similarly concludes that AI-using firms show positive productivity and non-negative employment effects but that aggregate effects remain too small to detect.[49][62]
20	
21	## Employment and Wage Dynamics: Displacement, Creation, and Polarization
22	
23	Across multiple data sources, AI exposure has so far produced no detectable net decline in aggregate employment—cross-country analysis of 23 OECD economies finds no clear relationship between AI exposure and employment growth, and U.S. survey estimates find only small wage effects in more exposed occupations with no significant effects on job openings or total jobs since ChatGPT's November 2022 release—while the disruption is instead materializing as distributional reallocation across skill groups, sectors, and demographic cohorts.[63][30][64] This null aggregate result for AI contrasts with the robot-exposure literature, which estimates that one more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42% across U.S. commuting zones.[65] Exposure is nonetheless broad, with one widely cited estimate suggesting around 80% of the U.S. workforce could see at least 10% of their tasks affected by LLMs and approximately 19% potentially having over half of their tasks impacted.[66] Forward-looking projections are starker than observed outcomes: PwC estimates that around 7% of existing UK jobs could be displaced within 5 years, rising to around 18% after 10 years and nearly 30% after 20 years, while one global synthesis projects 85 million jobs displaced by 2025 against 97 million new roles, a net creation of 12 million positions.[67][68] The incidence of AI is strongly skill-graded: Chinese provincial panel data from 2010–2019 show that a one-unit increase in AI raises high-skilled employment by 0.063% and medium-skilled employment by 0.001% while reducing low-skilled employment by 0.001%, a creation effect at the top paired with a replacement effect at the bottom.[69] Qualitative and systematic reviews converge on the same polarization pattern, finding that low-skilled routine workers face displacement and wage pressure, medium-skilled workers experience job polarization and reduced demand in conventional roles, and high-skilled workers gain through productivity improvements and new job creation.[70][71] Consistent with this, OECD analysis finds that the occupations most exposed to recent AI progress—Business Professionals, Managers, Chief Executives, and Science and Engineering Professionals—have nonetheless seen employment gains, whereas low-skilled workers face declining prospects and are often the most difficult to retrain when their tasks are automated.[64] Digital skill appears to be the decisive complementarity margin: across the 23 OECD countries studied, a one standard deviation increase in AI exposure is associated with 5.7 percentage points higher employment growth in high-computer-use occupations but with a 0.60 percentage point greater drop in usual weekly working hours (about 13 minutes per week) in low-computer-use occupations.[63] Wage dynamics mirror this gradient, with a 350-respondent cross-industry survey reporting wage growth of 8.7% for high-skilled workers versus 2.8% for low-skilled workers (p < 0.001), and wage growth significantly correlated with AI adoption level (β = 0.38), worker skill level (β = 0.42), and participation in AI-related training (β = 0.21).[72] Not all inequality effects widen dispersion, however: in a preregistered experiment on midlevel professional writing tasks, ChatGPT access raised output quality by 18% and decreased inequality between workers, implying generative AI can compress within-occupation productivity gaps.[73] Realized displacement is already measurable at the margin in online labor markets, where freelancers in occupations more exposed to generative AI experienced a 2% decline in the number of contracts and a 5% drop in earnings following the 2022 release of new AI software.[66][74] Vacancy data tell a similar story of adjustment through hiring rather than separations, with job-posting evidence showing a 12% decline in postings for highly AI-substitutable occupations, stronger negative impacts on entry-level roles, and hiring slowdowns instead of mass layoffs.[75] This hiring-margin adjustment is consistent with OECD firm-level evidence that employers rely on worker attrition rather than layoffs and that just 3.2% of U.S. firms used AI between 2016 and 2018, both of which help explain why displacement has not surfaced in aggregate employment statistics.[64] Complementing this, a large-scale field experiment found that AI-written job-post drafts made employers 19% more likely to post a vacancy but produced no discernible increase in matches, cautioning that rising vacancy volumes may overstate true labor demand.[76] Adoption itself is demographically skewed—generative AI is used most by younger, more highly educated, and higher-income workers and concentrated in customer service, marketing, and information technology—with U.S. worker adoption rising rapidly from 30.1% in December 2024 to 38.3% in December 2025.[30] Gender exposure is likewise asymmetric, with one analysis estimating that 58.87 million women in the U.S. workforce occupy positions highly exposed to AI automation compared with 48.62 million men.[68] At the firm level, the direction of the employment effect depends on the type of innovation: analysis of U.S. patents from 2007–2023 shows engagement-, learning-, and creativity-related AI augments human labor and raises productivity, whereas perception-related AI displaces labor and lowers operating costs.[77] Where AI is integrated into production processes, case-study evidence indicates it can reduce required labor hours by approximately 50% in affected tasks, with the saved hours redeployed to complementary work rather than eliminated.[78] Finally, demand for explicitly AI-skilled labor remains a thin slice of total labor demand, with AI-skill job postings averaging only 0.24% of all postings across occupations in 2019, up from 0.10% in 2012.[63]
24	
25	## Skill Transformation, Job Quality, and the Future of Work
26	
27	AI's labor-market impact has so far materialized primarily as task restructuring and changes in job quality within existing roles rather than mass job destruction: the OECD reports that, by workers' own accounts, "the greatest impact of AI has been felt mostly through changes in the tasks they perform in their current roles," and 83% of SMEs using generative AI report it has had no effect on overall staff need.[33][79] The World Economic Forum estimates cited in one review project that 50% of all employees will need reskilling by 2025, that over two-thirds of skills considered important in today's job requirements will change within five years, and that a third of essential 2025 skills will consist of technology competencies not yet regarded as crucial.[80] Reflecting this shift in skill demand, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics estimates cited in the same review project 19% job growth for computer and information research specialists and 20% for information security analysts by 2026.[80] A systematic review of 432 studies published since 2018, focusing on the 154 most relevant, finds AI simultaneously displacing routine, low-skilled work in factories, shops, and customer service centres while fuelling demand for experts in data science, cybersecurity, and AI ethics, and warns that displaced workers may struggle to transition into roles requiring advanced technical skills, deepening divides in income and opportunity.[81] By contrast, another analysis concludes that jobs remained through the machine-learning era, characterizes AI's workplace impact as "routine-biased technological deployment on steroids," and frames the coming choice as "augmentation" versus "simplistic automation" in which humans must remain "in the loop".[82] A rapid review of 20 primary studies published from 2020 onward finds the automation of repetitive and simple tasks already widespread among firms integrating AI, with adoption requiring a balanced development of hard and soft skills, and identifies gaps in digital skills, critical thinking, and self-management — compounded by financial resources, mindset, and a mismatch between graduate skills and job-market demands — as the main barriers to adoption.[83] Among the skills newly emerging atop priority lists are self-management competencies such as active learning, resilience, stress tolerance, and flexibility.[80] In software engineering specifically, reviewed studies raise the prospect of "software 2.0," in which neural networks may replace human-performed programming, forcing software engineers to adapt their roles.[83] Exposure to AI is skill-biased upward: UK analysis finds early-career employees with higher qualifications typically work in more AI-exposed occupations, with the professional, scientific and technical industry averaging an AIOE score of 0.86 versus -0.06 for veterinary activities, and female students in training that leads to more AI-exposed jobs (exposure score 0.34 compared to 0.06 for males).[84] By subject, exposure is highest for accounting and finance (AIOE 1.22 at Level 4/5 in further education and 1.31 via apprenticeships) and for economics (1.26) and mathematics and statistics (1.15) at Level 7+ in higher education, while building and construction, manufacturing technologies, and transportation operations and maintenance rank among the least exposed.[84] The workforce with AI skills remains small — at most 0.3% of those employed in OECD countries on average, though growing rapidly — and in EU countries nearly half of the AI workforce holds labour earnings in the top two deciles of the earnings distribution.[33] Wage evidence is mixed even within a single OECD review: one cited analysis finds a one-standard-deviation increase in AI exposure associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth, while the OECD's own AI implementation case studies find that AI has so far led to little wage change.[33] At the firm level, a one-standard-deviation increase in a resume-based measure of AI investment over an 8-year period corresponds to a 20.3% increase in sales, a 21.7% increase in employment, and a 22.4% increase in market valuation among U.S. firms, yet with no associated changes in sales per worker or total factor productivity.[33] On job quality, workers who use AI report higher enjoyment in their jobs, and 63% of AI users in finance and manufacturing in the OECD AI surveys reported that AI had improved their job satisfaction.[33] The mechanisms cut both ways, as AI can reduce tedious or dangerous tasks but may also leave workers with a higher-paced environment, and using AI to support or replace managers' tasks can reduce subordinates' privacy and autonomy while increasing work intensity and stress.[33] Equity concerns are concrete: men — particularly in finance — were more likely than women to expect a wage increase and less likely to expect a wage decrease due to AI, indicating that AI may put further pressure on existing wage inequalities.[33] Experimental evidence converges on generative AI compressing productivity gaps: in an experiment with 444 college-educated professionals, ChatGPT reduced time taken on writing tasks by 0.8 standard deviations and improved output quality by 0.4 standard deviations, with lower-ability workers benefiting more, and programmers using generative AI completed tasks over 50% faster than a control group, with benefits accruing most to the least experienced programmers.[85][33] The same writing experiment found that ChatGPT primarily served as a substitute for worker effort rather than augmenting worker skills.[85] In a survey of 366 employees in China, both cognitive and social uses of generative AI tools significantly enhanced innovative job performance — cognitive use primarily facilitating knowledge transfer and social use bolstering resource acquisition — with the resulting gains in job satisfaction pivotal in driving innovative performance.[86] Globally, the share of total employment in occupations with high augmentation potential ranges from 10.2 percent in Sub-Saharan Africa to 16.1 percent in Southeastern Asia and the Pacific.[87] Job quality at the lower end of the AI data supply chain is poor: U.S. online platform workers performing this work regularly report median earnings of roughly $2–$3 per hour, well below the federal minimum wage of $7.25.[87] Reskilling capacity lags the imperative: 94% of business leaders now expect employees to pick up new skills on the job, yet the European Commission estimates that less than two in five adults participate in learning every year in the EU.[80] A PRISMA-based systematic review that screened 639 papers published between March 2020 and March 2023, retaining 103 on the professional dimension and 35 on the personal dimension, documents persistent difficulties associated with the ongoing requirement for upskilling or reskilling as an adaptive reaction to technological change.[88] SMEs are adapting pragmatically: among generative-AI-using SMEs that experienced a skill gap, 39% say the technology helped compensate for it, rising to 46% where generative AI improved employee performance, while a third report reduced workload, 14% report reduced reliance on external contractors, and non-using SMEs are optimistic it could help address skill gaps (42%) and worker shortages (24%).[79] Educational alignment is a bottleneck, with studies documenting gaps between European universities' offerings and industry demands in AI, IoT, and edge computing, and a shortage of digital skills for Industry 4.0 smart factories that requires a dedicated skills framework for the Factory of the Future.[83] AI itself is part of the training response, as AI systems can intelligently identify and address individual skill gaps to facilitate efficient upskilling and reskilling, alongside rising demand for foundational AI literacy and ethics education.[89] Reviews converge that retraining must be paired with policy — including educational reforms, strategies to ensure AI-fueled growth is shared fairly, and collaboration among governments, industries, and universities — and that life-long learning should become part of an organization's strategic goals.[81][80]
28	
29	## Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions
30	
31	The central cross-study finding is a persistent gap between large, replicable task-level productivity gains and near-undetectable aggregate labor-market disruption: controlled RCTs report productivity gains on the order of 20–60% and field experiments 15–30%, yet measures of exposure, automation, and augmentation "show no sign of being related to changes in employment or unemployment", no systematic unemployment increase has appeared for highly exposed workers since late 2022, and administrative records across 11 exposed occupations show essentially zero effects on earnings or hours through 2024.[90][47][31][91] Ex-ante exposure measures themselves converge directionally toward high-wage, cognitively demanding work—AI-specific measures from Webb (2020), Felten et al. (2021), and Eloundou et al. (2024) correlate positively with wages, ranging from 0.27 to 0.54, with Felten et al. (2021) showing the strongest correlation at 0.54 —a pattern echoed by Anthropic's finding that the most exposed workers earn 47% more on average and include graduate-degree holders at 17.4% versus 4.5% of the unexposed group, and by the Burning Glass Institute/SHRM conclusion that GenAI will have its greatest impact on high-skilled, professional work long considered safe from roboticization.[90][31][92] The measures diverge sharply in magnitude depending on construction, however: Eloundou et al. estimate that at least 90 percent of computer programming tasks can be automated or augmented by LLMs, an exposure score of 0.9, while Anthropic's usage-based measure assigns computer programmers a score of just 0.061, reflecting that 6.1 percent of Claude queries map to programming tasks.[41] Anthropic's own comparison confirms this capability-usage gap, reporting that 97% of tasks observed in its Economic Index usage data fall into categories rated theoretically feasible by Eloundou et al., yet actual coverage remains a fraction of what is feasible, with fully feasible (β=1) tasks accounting for 68% of usage.[31] Where ex-post effects do appear, two independent sources converge on entry-level concentration: one review finds effects concentrated in entry-level segments of highly exposed occupations while senior employment remains largely stable, and Anthropic finds suggestive evidence that hiring of younger workers has slowed in exposed occupations.[91][31] Within occupations, controlled studies consistently document "skill compression," with disproportionately large benefits for less-experienced workers—GitHub Copilot users completed tasks 55.8% faster with larger gains among less experienced developers, nearly 5,000 developers across three field experiments showed a 26.08% increase in weekly task completion driven by junior staff, and lower-skilled translators realized gains roughly four times larger than higher-skilled peers, whose completion time fell 12.3% and earnings per minute rose 16.1% on average.[91] The distributional picture is less benign across countries and gender: using employment data from 59 countries, one analysis finds only 0.4% of jobs in low-income countries carry high substitution potential versus 5.5% in high-income countries, lower-middle-income countries hold the highest complementary share at 14.4% of employment, and within high-income countries 8.5% of female employment is classified as high automation potential compared to 3.9% for men.[90] Macro-level magnitude projections remain an unresolved contradiction: Acemoglu's parameterization implies AI raises total factor productivity by less than 0.66% over 10 years, while Erdem and Birur (2025) estimate rapid adoption could raise U.S. GDP by about $2.48 trillion by 2030 and another projection cited alongside them reaches roughly $7 trillion in annual global GDP over 10 years; a separate review similarly brackets the disagreement, citing a 0.5–0.6% macro-level productivity boost alongside estimates that up to 30% of current jobs could be affected by AI automation by 2030.[91][93] Forecasting humility is reinforced by the historical record: a prominent offshorability measure identified roughly a quarter of US jobs as vulnerable, yet a decade on most maintained healthy employment growth, and Yale's Budget Lab notes that the highest-exposure group (scores above 0.8) comprises only around 2% of workers with no observable employment trend.[31][47] The overall synthesis across reviews is that AI's labor-market effect is more complicated than either "doomsayers" or "optimists" assert, with outcomes hinging on the balance of benefits and harms across tasks, skills, occupations, people, places, and time.[41]
32	
33	## Conclusions and Research Gaps
34	
35	The evidence collectively supports a restructuring of labor demand that is concentrated in substitutable tasks and specific worker segments rather than an economy-wide employment collapse: current measures of AI exposure, automation, and augmentation show no sign of being related to changes in employment or unemployment, even as U.S. job postings in high-AI-substitution occupations declined approximately 5% soon after ChatGPT's release, deepening to around 10% after mid-2023, 15% by the end of 2024, and roughly 20% by the second quarter of 2025.[47][3] Multiple Current Population Survey–based analyses (Gimbel et al., Eckhardt and Goldschlag, and Dominski and Lee, all 2025) similarly find at most small changes in hiring in AI-exposed jobs, while the "Canaries in the Coal Mine" study finds effects concentrated on entry-level workers, indicating that aggregate stability masks uneven incidence.[94] Leading syntheses therefore characterize the overall evidence as inconclusive and claims of harmful impacts on particular groups as premature, arguing that research is "still in the first inning" because labor-demand findings conflict, current data are weak signals of the future, and labor demand is only one corner of the relevant research landscape.[95] Historical U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics data likewise offer little support for a structural break with pre-AI trends, noting that a Frey-and-Osborne-style 47% decline in jobs over 20 years would represent a dramatic departure from observed occupational dynamics, in which only 21 occupations representing 1% of 1999 jobs declined by 50 percent or more per decade between 1999 and 2018.[96] Micro-level evidence nonetheless confirms real restructuring where substitution is feasible: demand for substitutable freelance skill clusters fell significantly after ChatGPT's launch (difference-in-differences coefficient of −0.28 on log job postings) while complementary clusters were largely unaffected, and generative AI assistance raised customer-support resolutions per hour by 13.8% with agent and tenure fixed effects, with gains skewed toward lower-skilled workers in a pattern that contrasts with earlier skill-biased technical change.[97][50] Macro implications remain contested: one analysis reports Acemoglu's (2024) estimate that generative AI will add only 0.07% per year to growth over the coming decade, while a Federal Reserve speech cites a recent estimate that AI could contribute between 0.3 and 0.9 of a percentage point to annual total factor productivity growth over the next decade.[98][99] A first critical gap is measurement: exposure indices diverge so sharply that computer programmers score 0.9 under the Eloundou et al. task-based LLM measure but only 0.061 under Anthropic's Claude-usage measure, even though the underlying Eloundou et al. framework implies only 1.8% of U.S. tasks can be fully automated while 18.5% of workers have more than 50% of tasks exposed.[41][97] Existing exposure measures have historically lacked validation against real-world outcomes, though Tomlinson et al. (2025) found the Eloundou et al. predictions correlate highly with 2025 Microsoft Copilot usage, and the Yale Budget Lab concludes simply that better data are needed.[94][47] A second gap is general-equilibrium and matching-market effects: many plausible channels of labor-market impact remain under-explored relative to labor demand, and evidence on employer–candidate matching is ambiguous, with algorithmic resume assistance causally raising hiring and wages (van Inwegen et al.[95] 2025) and AI-assisted interviewing improving candidate selection (Aka et al. 2025), yet AI-improved cover letters becoming less informative signals of ability so that employers shift toward alternative signals such as past reviews (Cui et al. 2025).[94] A third gap concerns regional and international disparities: the IMF documents wide heterogeneity, with the employment share in high-exposure, high-complementarity occupations ranging from 20.2 to 37.3 percent in advanced economies while low-income countries carry 54–96.1 percent of employment in low-exposure occupations, and the ILO notes that the digital divide currently mutes impacts in developing economies while also blocking them from AI's benefits.[100][101] The China-shock literature cited in the Hamilton Project analysis warns that disruptions are especially challenging when geographically concentrated, since state-level shocks tend to persist, yet comparable subnational analyses of AI exposure remain largely absent.[95] Adjustment capacity is also poorly understood: average yearly occupation-switching probabilities are 43.7 percent in Brazil and 29.8 percent in the UK for college-educated workers and 38 and 27 percent for non-college-educated workers, but how such mobility maps onto AI-driven transitions is unresolved.[100] Distributional blind spots extend to gender, with the share of jobs at high automation potential in high-income countries standing at 9.6% for women versus 3.5% for men in 2025, up from 7.8% and 2.9% in 2023, and to job quality, since the discourse on job losses neglects wages, working conditions, and rights.[102][101] Finally, the policy-design challenge is that technological change through AI could have substantial economic impacts but, without deliberate policy interventions, workers might not share in those gains, and policymaking must navigate explicit trade-offs to maximize generative AI's potential to reduce socioeconomic inequalities while mitigating its harmful effects —priorities that reinforce the case for better data and sustained re-evaluation as adoption deepens.[41][103][95][47]
36	
37	## Limitations
38	
39	Limitations: The corpus is skewed toward lower-tier and unclassified material — only 4% of sources are T1 primary studies and just 1% are T2, while T6 material accounts for 21% and fully 25% of sources remain UNKNOWN — so the pipeline's outputs rest on a thin high-quality evidence base. The telemetry for this run records no detected cross-source contradictions and no screened not-comparable numeric pairings; the absence of flagged conflicts should therefore not be read as consensus, only as a lack of adjudicated disagreement. Because the telemetry surfaces no date range for the corpus, the evidence horizon cannot be characterized, and there is no way to confirm whether the synthesis reflects current findings or stale material. Until the 25% UNKNOWN share is classified and temporal coverage is established, downstream conclusions should be treated as hypothesis-generating rather than confirmatory.
